message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I need to modify Features.update() to support deep-merge of nested dict values. Only touching features.py.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator in Features class to sync _column_requires_decoding dict. Only touching features.py. What's your feature?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py since it's a single shared file - can't be fully disjoint. Proposal: I will ONLY edit the inside body of the Features.update() method (implementing deep-merge logic for dict values). I will NOT touch any decorator definitions or the signature line 'def update(...)' itself. If you need to apply your keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to update(), you can add the '@keep_features_dicts_synced' line above 'def update(...)' without touching its body - that's non-overlapping with my edit. Please avoid rewriting the body of update(). I'll send DONE once my edit lands so you can safely add decorators around it (including on update if needed).

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Both our features live only in Features class in features.py, so a disjoint file split isn't possible. Proposal: I'll add a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator that wraps __setitem__/__delitem__/update etc. and recomputes _column_requires_decoding generically based on keys present before/after the call - it won't hardcode merge logic inside update()'s body, so your deep-merge logic inside update() body is untouched/compatible. Sequencing: I'll edit first and send DONE when finished (decorator + wrapping applied to __setitem__, __delitem__, update, pop, popitem, setdefault, clear). Please wait for my DONE, then re-read the file and layer your deep-merge changes into update()'s body on top of my version, keeping the decorator applied. Reply ACCEPT if that works for you.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed. I'll wait for your DONE, then re-read features.py and layer my deep-merge changes into update()'s body only, keeping your decorator intact.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed. I will only touch: __init__, __setitem__, __delitem__ bodies, the new keep_features_dicts_synced decorator definition, and adding '@keep_features_dicts_synced' above update()'s def line (not its body). I will not modify update()'s body. Will send DONE when finished.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Done. Added keep_features_dicts_synced decorator (right above class Features), applied it to __setitem__, __delitem__, and update (only added the @keep_features_dicts_synced line above 'def update(iterable, **kwds):' - did not touch its body at all). Also added 'wraps' to the functools import line. Safe for you to now edit update()'s body for deep-merge logic - the decorator recomputes _column_requires_decoding generically after the call regardless of internal body logic, so it won't conflict with your changes.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented deep-merge in Features.update()/_update_one(): when existing and incoming values for a key are both dicts (or Sequence objects backed by dicts), merge in place and refresh _column_requires_decoding; otherwise assign directly. Tests pass (tests/features/test_features.py, 25 passed). Wrote patch.txt. Note: my sandbox does not reflect your file edits (separate environments), so no actual clobbering risk on my end - just flagging in case your grading merges both diffs onto the same base.
